relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jesus sees a human born blind.
The disciples ask through inherited blame logic.
Jesus refuses victim-blame and opens the frame of revealed works.
Day is the time to work the Sender’s works.
While in the world, he is the world’s light.
Clay and touch meet the eyes.
The Sent pool becomes the place where washing opens sight.
Neighbors question the transformed beggar’s identity.
He answers simply: I am he.
They ask how the eyes opened.
He tells the concrete story: clay, Siloam, washing, sight.
He does not yet know where Jesus is.
The formerly blind one is brought to the Pharisees.
Sabbath frames the controversy.
He repeats the healing story plainly.
Some judge Sabbath-breaking; others see the sign. Division opens.
The healed one names Jesus prophet.
They resist trusting the healed sight.
Parents are questioned about birth-blindness and sight.
They affirm what they know: he is their son, born blind.
Fear makes them defer the witness to him.
Synagogue-expulsion pressure shapes their answer.
Ask him, they say again.
The authorities try to force the healed one into their verdict.
He holds to the one thing he knows: I was blind, now I see.
They demand the method again.
He exposes their refusal to hear.
They insult him and claim Moses.
They know Moses but refuse this one’s origin.
The marvel is their ignorance before opened eyes.
He reasons from divine hearing and reverent will-doing.
No one has heard of opening eyes from birth-blindness.
The work points to divine origin.
They return to blame and cast him out.
Jesus finds the one expelled by religious power.
The healed one asks who the Son of Humanity is.
The one seen and speaking is he.
Trust becomes worship.
Judgment reverses sight and blindness.
The Pharisees ask if they too are blind.
Claimed sight without truth keeps misalignment remaining.
